name: Moral Foundations Theory (Haidt)
core_claim: Human moral judgment runs on a small set of innate, intuitive, evolutionarily-prepared psychological systems ('foundations'), each tuned to a recurrent adaptive challenge, on top of which cultures build their specific virtues, narratives and institutions. The classic five are Care/Harm (compassion, protecting the vulnerable), Fairness/Cheating (reciprocity, justice, cheating), Loyalty/Betrayal (in-group solidarity, sacrifice, treason), Authority/Subversion (respect for legitimate hierarchy, tradition, rebellion) and Sanctity/Degradation (purity, the sacred, disgust, the body, contamination). A sixth, Liberty/Oppression (resentment of domination and coercion, resistance to bullies and tyrants), was added in The Righteous Mind (2012). MFT's central, transferable claim for a narrative engine is twofold: (1) morality is PLURAL, not a single good-vs-evil axis — a story carries a distinctive MORAL TEXTURE depending on WHICH foundations its conflicts activate; and (2) people and groups differ systematically in which foundations they weight, so the SAME moral content lands differently on different viewers. Politically, the standard finding (Graham, Haidt & Nosek 2009) is that progressives rely heavily on Care and Fairness (the 'individualizing' foundations) and lightly on the rest, while conservatives rely on all foundations more evenly (adding the 'binding' foundations Loyalty/Authority/Sanctity). This explains cross-cultural and cross-political variation in narrative appeal that valence/arousal emotion taxonomies are structurally blind to: two viewers can feel the same arousal at a story of a soldier defying orders, yet one reads it as heroic (Liberty/Care) and the other as a betrayal (Loyalty/Authority).
descriptive_vs_experiential: Primarily VALUES-BASED / motivational-moral, sitting at a level distinct from both topical-descriptive and affective-experiential taxonomies. It is not about what the story is literally ABOUT (settings, plots) nor purely about the felt emotion (joy, fear). It characterises the MORAL DIMENSIONS along which a narrative's conflicts are structured — which moral concerns are at stake and which way the story comes down on them. It is closest to a 'moral-texture' or 'values' layer: it answers 'what kind of moral problem is this, and whose values does it flatter or offend?'. It is partly descriptive (a synopsis can reveal that the conflict is about purity/contamination vs. loyalty), partly relational (whether that texture RESONATES depends on the viewer's own foundation weights). It is the bridge between content and a viewer's identity/values, which is exactly the dimension hedonic/eudaimonic and circumplex-emotion frameworks omit.
key_dimensions: Six bipolar foundations, each a virtue/vice pair, treated as continuous intensity scores per work: (1) Care / Harm — protecting vs. harming the vulnerable; compassion, suffering, cruelty, nurturance. (2) Fairness / Cheating — reciprocity, justice, desert, exploitation, free-riding. The MFQ-2 (Atari et al. 2023) SPLITS this into two distinct foundations: Equality (equal outcomes/treatment, anti-discrimination) and Proportionality (reward in proportion to contribution, merit, just deserts) — relevant because revenge and 'getting what you deserve' stories load Proportionality, not Equality. (3) Loyalty / Betrayal — group solidarity, sacrifice for the team/nation/family, treason, defection. (4) Authority / Subversion — respect for hierarchy, tradition, duty, legitimate order vs. rebellion, insubordination. (5) Sanctity / Degradation (Purity) — the sacred, cleanliness, the body, disgust, contamination, taboo, transcendence vs. defilement. (6) Liberty / Oppression — autonomy, resistance to domination, anti-tyranny, anti-bullying. A standard higher-order grouping: INDIVIDUALIZING (Care + Fairness) vs. BINDING (Loyalty + Authority + Sanctity), with Liberty cross-cutting. Per work the candidate axes are: care_score, fairness_score (or equality + proportionality), loyalty_score, authority_score, sanctity_score, liberty_score, each in both virtue (foundation upheld/celebrated) and vice (foundation violated) polarity, plus a derived individualizing-vs-binding tilt.
cross_cultural_validity: MFT was explicitly BUILT as a cross-cultural theory (its origin is anthropological — Shweder's 'big three' ethics of autonomy/community/divinity — and Haidt's fieldwork in Brazil and India), so its ambition is strong; its measurement history is more mixed. The original Moral Foundations Questionnaire (MFQ-1) factor structure did NOT replicate cleanly outside WEIRD samples — the five-factor model fit poorly in several non-Western cultures, and the individualizing/binding split is sharpest in politically-polarised Western (especially US) samples. This is a documented, real limitation. The MFQ-2 (Atari, Haidt et al. 2023) was developed specifically to fix this: built and validated across roughly 25 cultures, it splits Fairness into Equality and Proportionality and shows substantially better and more invariant six-factor fit cross-culturally; the World Values Survey community has begun adopting it, and 2024-2025 validations exist for Germany, Turkey, Iran, Ghana and others (with some still reporting weaker fit for binding subscales in particular contexts). Net: the THEORY travels well as a pluralistic frame; the SPECIFIC foundation set and especially the political individualizing/binding interpretation are most robust in Western/polarised contexts, and the CONTENT of each foundation (what counts as sacred, who is the legitimate in-group) is heavily culturally variable. For a film recommender this is a feature, not only a bug: cross-cultural and cross-political variation in appeal is precisely what MFT is designed to capture.
empirical_grounding: Among the better-grounded value frameworks, with directly relevant film/narrative evidence. (1) Psychometric/factor-analytic: large MFQ and MFQ-2 datasets across many cultures (Atari et al. 2023, 25 cultures). (2) Predictive/correlational: foundation profiles reliably predict political ideology, religiosity, attitudes (Graham, Haidt & Nosek 2009 and a large downstream literature). (3) Computational text analysis: the Moral Foundations Dictionary (MFD, MFD 2.0) and the crowd-sourced Extended Moral Foundations Dictionary (eMFD, Hopp et al. 2021) operationalise foundations as extractable from text, and a word-embedding method has been applied directly to AUTOMATICALLY ASSIGN moral foundations to MOVIES (Sci. ScienceDirect / Knowledge-Based Systems 2023). (4) Narrative/film-specific empirical results: Grizzard-style and children's-movie studies (e.g. PLOS ONE 2021, Eden/Grizzard line of work) coded heroes vs. villains and found heroes uphold individualizing foundations more and binding less than villains, and that political conservatism predicts more favourable evaluation of binding-promoting messages; and an analysis of ~1.6 million IMDb reviews (Journal of Media Psychology 2025) found moral salience in reviews strongly associated with film SUCCESS, with Care, Fairness, Loyalty and Authority positively related to audience ratings, popularity and revenue. So there is correlational evidence linking foundation content to actual audience appeal and box office — the closest thing to the enjoyment/preference validation HelpME2C cares about. What is missing: causal, per-viewer matching evidence (does aligning a viewer's foundation profile to a film's profile predict THAT viewer's completion?). To validate LLM-extracted foundation scores you would need gold human foundation codings of synopses (eMFD-style), correlate LLM scores against them, and then test whether film foundation profile crossed with a viewer's MFQ-2 profile predicts that viewer's ratings/completion better than genre alone.
operationalisability: MODERATELY to GOOD from a synopsis, better than most affect frameworks because moral conflict is often the LITERAL subject of a logline. An LLM can usually detect from a synopsis that a story centres on betrayal (Loyalty/vice), revenge/just-deserts (Proportionality), resistance to a tyrant (Liberty + Authority/vice), self-sacrifice for the group (Loyalty/virtue), defilement/taboo/contamination (Sanctity/vice), or protecting the vulnerable (Care/virtue). Existing computational precedent (MFD 2.0, eMFD, movie word-embedding assignment) shows lexical/embedding extraction is feasible at scale, and an LLM should exceed bag-of-words dictionaries. Extraction unit: WHOLE WORK is natural for foundation salience; ARC-level is valuable for serialised TV (a season can pivot from Care to Loyalty conflicts); CHARACTER-level is the most powerful and is supported by the hero/villain coding literature (which foundations the protagonist vs. antagonist embody). Failure modes: (1) Polarity confusion — is the foundation UPHELD or VIOLATED? A revenge film foregrounds Proportionality but may ultimately condemn it; the synopsis may not reveal the moral verdict. (2) Whose side — a synopsis may not say whether the rebel is framed as heroic (Liberty) or as a dangerous subversive (Authority), and that judgment is exactly the receiver-dependent part. (3) Sanctity/Purity is easy to miss when it is aesthetic/atmospheric (body-horror, religious awe) rather than stated. (4) Marketing copy moralises everything ('a fight for justice') inflating Care/Fairness reads. (5) Binding foundations (Loyalty/Authority/Sanctity) are under-verbalised in Western synopsis conventions and risk under-detection — a systematic bias. (6) Sarcastic/satirical or morally-ambiguous works (anti-heroes, prestige drama) carry CONFLICTING foundation signals that a single score flattens. Recommend extracting per-foundation virtue AND vice salience plus a confidence flag, and treating output as moral-texture prior, not verdict.
motivation_served: Maps to IDENTITY EXPRESSION and VALUES-AFFIRMATION motivations, and to the moral/elevation strand of eudaimonic appreciation (being moved by virtue, outraged by injustice, awed by sacrifice or the sacred). It is less about hedonic fun or mood-repair and more about whether a story's moral world FEELS RIGHT or affirms/challenges the viewer's sense of how things ought to be. For GROUP viewing this is one of the most decision-relevant frameworks: moral mismatch is a frequent, often unspoken source of joint-viewing friction (one partner finds a vigilante satisfying, the other finds it morally ugly; one is moved by a patriotic war film, the other by an anti-war one). Because MFT predicts SYSTEMATIC, PROFILE-LEVEL differences (e.g. along the political/binding-vs-individualizing axis) it lets a two-person recommender (a) detect when two viewers' foundation profiles diverge, (b) prefer works whose moral texture sits in shared territory or is morally ambiguous enough to be readable both ways, and (c) explicitly avoid works that strongly affirm one viewer's foundations while offending the other's. It directly serves the North Star: moral-texture matching is a concrete lever for finding shows two people both want to watch rather than merely tolerate.
receiver_dependence: STRONGLY MIXED, and the split is the whole point of the framework for a two-person recommender. The WORK has a fairly objective foundation PROFILE — which foundations its conflicts activate and which polarity (this revenge thriller foregrounds Proportionality-violation-then-restoration; this drama centres Sanctity/defilement). That profile is reasonably stable across viewers and IS extractable from synopsis (an absolute property, like a content tag). But the VALENCE/APPEAL of that profile is RELATIONAL: whether a Loyalty-celebrating, Authority-respecting war film reads as stirring or as jingoistic, whether a transgressive Sanctity-violating story reads as liberating or repellent, depends on the VIEWER's own foundation weights (their MFQ-2 profile, broadly tracking the individualizing/binding and political axis). So encode TWO separate things: (a) WORK-level foundation salience scores (six foundations x virtue/vice) — synopsis-extractable, roughly absolute. (b) VIEWER-level foundation weights — NOT extractable from any synopsis; derived from onboarding, ratings history, or an MFQ-2-style short instrument, and modelled per user. Matching = score the resonance between a viewer's weights and a work's profile; for pairs, find works whose profile resonates adequately with BOTH viewers' weights, or is ambiguous enough to be read favourably by both. This work-profile vs. viewer-weights separation mirrors the hedonic/eudaimonic tendency-vs-motivation split and is the key design takeaway.
blind_spots: (1) Content-of-foundation blindness — tells you a story is about Loyalty but not WHO is loyal to WHAT (nation, family, gang); it needs the topical taxonomy underneath it. (2) Verdict ambiguity — captures which foundations are AT STAKE but not cleanly how the narrative ADJUDICATES them; prestige drama deliberately refuses to resolve, and MFT scores flatten that. (3) Non-moral appeal invisible — spectacle, craft, humour, suspense mechanics, aesthetic beauty, parasocial attachment and sheer escapism carry no moral-foundation signal yet drive huge amounts of enjoyment. (4) Aesthetic/sensory and intellectual pleasures (puzzle of a mystery, formal cleverness) are off-frame. (5) WEIRD/political bias — the individualizing/binding interpretation and even the foundation set are sharpest in Western polarised samples; the framework can over-read US culture-war structure into films from other moral cultures. (6) Foundation completeness is contested — proposed-but-rejected candidates (honour, ownership, waste, equity) suggest the six may not be exhaustive; honour/face especially matters in many non-Western narratives and is under-served. (7) It says little about ENJOYMENT INTENSITY or rewatchability per se — moral salience predicts appeal on average but a morally inert comfort-watch can still be a favourite.
vocabulary_implications: Add a CROSS-CUTTING 'moral-texture' facet, ORTHOGONAL to the topical theme list — do not fold these into theme slugs. Proposed work-level fields, each a 0-1 salience score with a virtue/vice polarity (or two scores, *_upheld and *_violated): care, fairness (or split equality + proportionality per MFQ-2), loyalty, authority, sanctity, liberty. Plus a derived scalar individualizing_vs_binding_tilt (-1 fully binding ... +1 fully individualizing) which is the single most useful summary for group matching along the political/values axis. LLM-extractable from synopsis with a confidence flag; recommend also capturing per-protagonist vs. per-antagonist foundation embodiment where the hero/villain literature shows the strongest signal. Human-readable derived tags for UI/explanations: 'self-sacrifice-for-the-group' (Loyalty-virtue), 'betrayal-of-trust' (Loyalty-vice), 'just-deserts'/'they-get-what-they-deserve' (Proportionality), 'defying-tyranny' (Liberty), 'duty-vs-conscience' (Authority-vs-Care conflict), 'sacred-violated'/'taboo-broken' (Sanctity-vice), 'protecting-the-vulnerable' (Care-virtue). FLAG explicitly NOT synopsis-extractable and requiring different data sources: (a) the VIEWER's own foundation weights / MFQ-2-style profile — onboarding or behavioural, modelled per user; (b) the narrative's ultimate moral VERDICT when the synopsis is spoiler-free — may need critic text, full-script, or audience-review (eMFD-style) signals; (c) honour/face and other contested foundations not in the canonical six. Encode foundation scores as a probabilistic moral-texture prior fused with topical themes, genre and audience-review moral salience.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: MFT is largely ORTHOGONAL to the existing slug list (which is topical/structural) but it provides a clean MORAL LENS that explains, groups and sharpens a whole cluster of the existing Moral and Power/society slugs. Strong validations / near-direct mappings: 'betrayal-and-forgiveness' and 'complicity-with-evil' -> Loyalty (vice) + Care; 'vengeance-and-its-cost' and 'vigilante-justice' -> Proportionality + Liberty, with the 'its-cost' framing capturing exactly MFT's foundation-conflict; 'revolution-and-rebellion' and 'civilian-under-occupation' -> Liberty/Oppression + Authority/Subversion; 'institutional-failure', 'corruption-and-cover-up', 'power-corrupts', 'propaganda-and-truth' -> Authority + Fairness/Cheating; 'class-conflict' -> Equality (the MFQ-2 split) + Fairness; 'guilt-and-atonement', 'redemption-arc', 'moral-compromise', 'ethical-dilemma', 'impossible-choice' -> these are precisely FOUNDATION-CONFLICT slugs (Care-vs-Loyalty, duty-vs-conscience) and MFT gives them a principled internal structure; 'found-family' and 'soldier-coming-home'/'war-and-its-toll' -> Loyalty + Care. MISALIGNMENT flag: the Moral cluster slugs conflate the moral TOPIC with the moral DIMENSION and the moral VERDICT — MFT suggests decomposing them into (foundation x polarity x verdict) rather than single tokens. GAPS revealed: (1) no facet for SANCTITY/PURITY/taboo/defilement as a moral axis — currently only gestured at via 'folk-horror'/'cosmic-horror'/'existential-horror' (which carry sanctity-degradation undertones) and 'addiction-and-recovery'; worth an explicit purity/sanctity tag. (2) No explicit LOYALTY-vs-individual-conscience axis as a standalone facet. (3) Crucially, NO representation of the individualizing-vs-binding / political-moral tilt, which is the dimension that most predicts cross-political appeal divergence between two viewers — this is the single most valuable addition MFT recommends, and it belongs as a cross-cutting facet, not a theme slug.
key_sources: Haidt, J., & Joseph, C. (2004). 'Intuitive ethics: How innately prepared intuitions generate culturally variable virtues.' Daedalus 133(4) (foundational; free PDFs widely available). Graham, J., Haidt, J., & Nosek, B. A. (2009). 'Liberals and conservatives rely on different sets of moral foundations.' JPSP 96(5):1029-1046 (the political-differences result; free PDF on moralfoundations.org/publications). Graham, Haidt, Koleva, Motyl, Iyer, Wojcik & Ditto (2013). 'Moral Foundations Theory: The Pragmatic Validity of Moral Pluralism.' Advances in Experimental Social Psychology 47 (canonical review; free PDF: https://cpb-us-e2.wpmucdn.com/sites.uci.edu/dist/1/863/files/2020/06/Graham-et-al-2013.AESP_.pdf). Haidt, J. (2012). The Righteous Mind: Why Good People Are Divided by Politics and Religion (introduces Liberty foundation; book). Atari, M., Haidt, J., Graham, J., et al. (2023). 'Morality Beyond the WEIRD: How the Nomological Network of Morality Varies Across Cultures' / MFQ-2 development and 25-culture validation, JPSP (splits Fairness into Equality + Proportionality; ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/375166675). moralfoundations.org (hub, free; questionnaires + bibliography). Moral Foundations Dictionary 2.0 (Provalis/WordStat, free: https://provalisresearch.com/products/content-analysis-software/wordstat-dictionary/moral-foundations-dictionary/) and quanteda.dictionaries MFD. Hopp, F. R., Fisher, J. T., Cornell, D., Huskey, R., & Weber, R. (2021). 'The Extended Moral Foundations Dictionary (eMFD): Development and Applications of a Crowd-Sourced Approach to Extracting Moral Intuitions from Text.' Behavior Research Methods (crowd-sourced, text-extractable; ResearchGate: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/342137805). Automatic assignment of moral foundations to MOVIES by word embedding, Knowledge-Based Systems 2023 (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0950705123002897, paywalled). Tamborini/Eden/Grizzard line + 'Moral foundations theory, political identity, and the depiction of morality in children's movies,' PLOS ONE 2021 (open access: https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0248928). 'Moral Salience in Film Reviews Associated With Film Success: An Analysis of 1.6 Million Reviews,' Journal of Media Psychology 37(1), 2025 (https://econtent.hogrefe.com/doi/10.1027/1864-1105/a000416, paywalled). Wikipedia 'Moral foundations theory' (free overview). C-MFD 2.0 Chinese Moral Foundation Dictionary, Computational Communication Research 2023 (cross-cultural extraction).
